Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Algebra

Tags: Math Algebra Identity

Quadratic Formula

If

ax2+bx+c=0

then

x=2a−b±b2−4ac​​

2ex−e−x​

2ex+e−x​

The definition of

coshxsinhx​=ex+e−xex−e−x​

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

ex=…

Hyperbolic identity

coshx+sinhx

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

e−x=…

Hyperbolic identity

coshx−sinhx

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

1=…

Hyperbolic identity

cosh2x−sinh2x

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

sinx=…

Express as a hyperbolic function

−isinh(ix)=2ieix−e−ix​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

sinhx=…

Express as a trigonometric function

−isin(ix)=2ex−e−x​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

cosx=…

Express as a hyperbolic function

cosh(ix)=2eix+e−ix​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

coshx=…

Express as a trigonometric function

cos(ix)=2ex+e−x​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

Euler's Formula

eixe−ix​=cosx+isinx=cosx−isinx​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Trigonometry

Tags: Math Trigonometric Hyperbolic Identity

De Moivre's Formula

(cos(x)+isin(x))n(cosh(x)+isinh(x))n​=cos(nx)+isin(nx)=cosh(nx)+isinh(nx)​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Limits

The definition of e as a limit when n→∞

e=n→∞lim​(1+n1​)n

ex=n→∞lim​(1+nx​)n

e=n→0lim​(1+n)1/n

ex=n→0lim​(1+n)x/n

dxd​(secx)dxd​(cscx)dxd​(sechx)dxd​(cschx)​=secxtanx=−cscxcotx=−sechxtanhx=−cschxcothx​

1−x2​1​

Related

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of 1−x1​

1−x1​​=1+x+x2+x3+⋯=n=0∑∞​xn​

Valid for ∣x∣<1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of 1+x1​

1+x1​​=1−x+x2−x3+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−x)n​

Valid for ∣x∣<1

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ex

ex​=1+x+2!x2​+3!x3​+⋯=n=0∑∞​n!xn​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of e−x

e−x​=1−x+2!x2​−3!x3​+⋯=n=0∑∞​n!(−x)n​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ln(x)

ln(x)​=(x−1)−2(x−1)2​+3(x−1)3​−4(x−1)4​+⋯=n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1n(x−1)n​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ln(1−x)

ln(1−x)​=−x−2x2​−3x3​−4x4​−⋯=−n=1∑∞​nxn​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Taylor-Series

Taylor series expansion of ln(1+x)

ln(1+x)​=x−2x2​+3x3​−4x4​+⋯=n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1nxn​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Trigonometric Identity

Taylor series expansion of sin(x)

sin(x)​=x−3!x3​+5!x5​−7!x7​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−1)n(2n+1)!x2n+1​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Trigonometric Identity

Taylor series expansion of cos(x)

cos(x)​=1−2!x2​+4!x4​−6!x6​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−1)n(2n)!x2n​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Trigonometric Identity

Taylor series expansion of arctan(x)

arctan(x)​=x−3x3​+5x5​−7x7​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(−1)n2n+1x2n+1​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Hyperbolic Identity

Taylor series expansion of sinh(x)

sinh(x)​=x+3!x3​+5!x5​+7!x7​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(2n+1)!x2n+1​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Taylor Series

Tags: Math Calculus Taylor-Series Hyperbolic Identity

Taylor series expansion of cosh(x)

cosh(x)​=1+2!x2​+4!x4​+6!x6​+⋯=n=0∑∞​(2n)!x2n​​

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

s1​

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

(s−c)2−a2a​
L{ectsinh(at)}

Related

L{ectsin(at)}L{ectcos(at)}L{ectsinh(at)}L{ectcosh(at)}​=(s−c)2+a2a​=(s−c)2+a2s−c​=(s−c)2−a2a​=(s−c)2−a2s−c​​

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

L{ectcosh(at)}
(s−c)2−a2s−c​

Related

L{ectsin(at)}L{ectcos(at)}L{ectsinh(at)}L{ectcosh(at)}​=(s−c)2+a2a​=(s−c)2+a2s−c​=(s−c)2−a2a​=(s−c)2−a2s−c​​

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 2::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

(s−c)2−a2s−c​
L{ectcosh(at)}

Related

L{ectsin(at)}L{ectcos(at)}L{ectsinh(at)}L{ectcosh(at)}​=(s−c)2+a2a​=(s−c)2+a2s−c​=(s−c)2−a2a​=(s−c)2−a2s−c​​

Type: Basic Math (and reversed card)::Card 2::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Identities::Laplace Transforms

Tags: Math Calculus Identity Laplace-Transform

s2F(s)−sf(0)−f′(0)
L{f′′(t)}

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Limits

Tags: Math Calculus Limits Theory

What is a continuous function?

A function f(x) is continuous at a point x=c if:

  • f(c) exists: The function is defined at the point c.
  • limx→c​f(x) exists: The limit of the function as x approaches c from both the left and right sides is the same.
  • limx→c​f(x)=f(c): The limit of the function as x approaches c is equal to the actual value of the function at c.

If a function is continuous at every point in its domain, it is called a continuous function.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the extreme value theorem (EVT).

The extreme value theorem states that if a real-valued function f is continuous on the closed and bounded interval [a,b], then f must attain a maximum and a minimum, each at least once.

That is, there exist numbers c and d in [a,b] such that: f(c)≤f(x)≤f(d)∀x∈[a,b].

In simpler terms, a continuous function on a closed interval will always have a highest point and a lowest point.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the Rolle's theorem.

The Rolle's theorem is a special case of the mean value theorem.

The Rolle's theorem states that if a function f(x) is:

  • Continuous on a closed interval [a,b]
  • Differentiable on the open interval (a,b)
  • f(a)=f(b) (the function has the same value at the endpoints)

Then there exists at least one point c in the open interval (a,b) such that f′(c)=0.

Geometrically, this means if a continuous and differentiable curve starts and ends at the same height, there must be at least one point in between where the tangent line is horizontal (i.e., the slope is zero).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the mean value theorem (MVT).

The mean value theorem is a generalization of Rolle's theorem.

The mean value theorem states that if a function f(x) is:

  • Continuous on a closed interval [a,b]
  • Differentiable on the open interval (a,b)

Then there exists at least one point c in the open interval (a,b) such that the instantaneous rate of change (the derivative f′(c)) is equal to the average rate of change over the interval.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

f′(c)=b−af(b)−f(a)​

Or, alternatively:

f(c)=b−a1​∫ab​f(x)dx

The Rolle's theorem can be derived from the mean value theorem: if f(a)=f(b), then the right side of the mean value theorem equation becomes

f′(c)=b−af(a)−f(a)​=b−a0​

which means f′(c)=0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Differentiation

Tags: Math Calculus Differentiation Theory

The definition of the intermediate value theorem (IVT).

The intermediate value theorem states that if a function f(x) is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], and L is any value between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one number c in the interval [a,b] such that f(c)=L.

Intuitively, if you can draw the graph of a function without lifting your pencil, and it goes from one y-value to another, it must pass through all the y-values in between.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Integration

Tags: Math Calculus Integration Theory

What is the fundamental theorem of calculus (FTC)?

Consider a function f which is continuous on [a,b].

  • If F(x)=∫ax​(t)dt, then F′(x)=f(x) on (a,b).
  • If F is any antiderivative of f on [a,b] (i.e., F′(x)=f(x)), then ∫ab​f(x)dx=F(b)−F(a).

The fundamental theorem of calculus is arguably the most important theorem in elementary calculus, as it establishes the profound connection between differentiation and integration.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is an infinite sequence?

An infinite sequence is an ordered and indexed list of numbers of the form

a1​,a2​,a3​,…,an​,…

denoted as

{an​}n=1∞​

or simply

{an​}.

Each number an​ is a term of the sequence.

The subscript n is the index variable of the sequence.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the different ways to define a sequence?

An infinite sequence can be defined in various ways:

  • By listing terms {2,4,6,8,…}.
  • By an explicit formula that defines the n-th term directly in terms of n, an​=f(n).
  • By a recurrence relation, a rule that defines a term based on previous terms in the sequence, an​=f(an−1​,an−2​,…).

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the limit of a sequence?

A sequence {an​} converges to a real number L if for all ϵ>0, there exists an integer N such that ∣an​−L∣<ϵ if n≥N. The number L is the limit of the sequence and we write

n→∞lim​an​=L or an​→L.

In this case, we say the sequence {an​} is a convergent sequence. If a sequence does not converge, it is a divergent sequence, and we say the limit does not exist.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the squeeze theorem for sequences?

Consider sequences {an​}, {bn​}, and {cn​}. Suppose there exists an integer N such that an​≤bn​≤cn​ for all n≥N. If there exists a real number L such that

n→∞lim​an​=L=n→∞lim​cn​,

then {bn​} converges and

n→∞lim​bn​=L

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is a bounded sequence?

A sequence {an​} is bounded above if there exists a real number M such that

an​≤M

for all positive integers n.

A sequence {an​} is bounded below if there exists a real number M such that

M≤an​

for all positive integers n.

A sequence {an​} is a bounded sequence if it is bounded above and bounded below.

If a sequence is not bounded, it is an unbounded sequence.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the relationship between convergent and bounded sequences?

If a sequence {an​} converges, then it is bounded.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is a monotone sequence?

A sequence {an​} is increasing for all n≥n0​ if

an​≤an+1​ for all n≥n0​.

A sequence {an​} is decreasing for all n≥n0​ if

an​≥an+1​ for all n≥n0​.

A sequence {an​} is a monotone sequence for all n≥n0​ if it is increasing for all n≥n0​ or decreasing for all n≥n0​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the monotone convergence theorem?

If {an​} is a bounded sequence and there exists a positive integer n0​ such that {an​} is monotone for all n≥n0​, then {an​} converges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is an infinite series?

An infinite series is a sum of the terms of an infinite sequnce an​:

n=1∑∞​an​=a1​+a2​+a3​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the harmonic series?

The harmonic series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​n1​

The harmonic series is a special case of the p-series with p=1.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the harmonic series?

The harmonic series ∑n=1∞​n1​ diverges to infinity.

Related:

The alternate harmonic series ∑n=1∞​(−1)n+1n1​ converges to ln2.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the alternating harmonic series?

The alternating harmonic series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1n1​=1−21​+31​−41​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the alternate harmonic series?

The alternate harmonic series ∑n=1∞​(−1)n+1n1​ converges to ln2.

Related:

The harmonic series ∑n=1∞​n1​ diverges to infinity.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the geometric series?

The geometric series is an infinite series of the following form

n=0∑∞​arn=a+ar+ar2+ar3+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the geometric series?

The geometric series ∑n=0∞​arn converges to 1−ra​ if ∣r∣<1, otherwise it diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the p-series?

The p-series series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​np1​=1p1​+2p1​+3p1​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of the p-series?

The p-series ∑n=1∞​np1​ converges if p>1, otherwise it diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the telescoping series?

The telescoping series is an infinite series of the following form

n=1∑∞​an​−an+1​.

In other words, the telescoping series is a series in which most of the terms cancel in each of the partial sums, leaving only some of the first terms and some of the last terms.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the algebraic properties of convergent series?

Let ∑n=1∞​an​ and ∑n=1∞​bn​ be convergent series. Then the following algebraic properties hold.

  • Sum Rule: The series ∑n=1∞​(an​+bn​) converges and ∑n=1∞​(an​+bn​)=∑n=1∞​an​+∑n=1∞​bn​.
  • Difference Rule: The series ∑n=1∞​(an​−bn​) converges and ∑n=1∞​(an​−bn​)=∑n=1∞​an​−∑n=1∞​bn​.
  • Constant Multiple Rule: For any real number c, the series ∑n=1∞​can​ converges and ∑n=1∞​can​=c∑n=1∞​an​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the divergence test for a series?

Consider the sequence an​. If limn→∞​an​=0 or limn→∞​an​ does not exist, then the series ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.

The converse is not true. That is, if limn→∞​an​=0, we cannot make any conclusion about the convergence of ∑n=1∞​an​.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the integral test?

Suppose ∑n=0∞​an​ is a series with positive terms an​. Suppose there exists a function f and a positive integer N such that the following three conditions are satisfied:

  • f is continuous,
  • f is decreasing, and
  • f(n)=an​ for all integers n≥N.

Then ∑n=1∞​an​ and ∫N∞​f(x)dx both converge or both diverge.

Although convergence of the integral implies convergence of the related series, it does not imply that the value of the integral and the series are the same.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

How to estimate the value of a series?

TODO

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

How to estimate the remainder of a series?

TODO

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the direct comparison test?

Suppose there exists an integer N such that 0≤an​≤bn​ for all n≥N. If ∑n=1∞​bn​ converges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges.

Suppose there exists an integer N such that an​≥bn​≥0 for all n≥N. If ∑n=1∞​bn​ diverges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the limit comparison test?

Let an​, bn​≥0 for all n≥1.

If limn→∞​an​/bn​=L=0, then ∑n=1∞​an​ and ∑n=1∞​bn​ both converge or both diverge.

If limn→∞​an​/bn​=0 and ∑n=1∞​bn​ converges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges.

If limn→∞​an​/bn​=∞ and ∑n=1∞​bn​ diverges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the alternating series?

The alternating series is a sum of the terms of an infinite sequnce an​ whose terms alternate between positive and negative values:

n=1∑∞​(−1)n+1an​=a1​−a2​+a3​−…

or

n=1∑∞​(−1)nan​=−a1​+a2​−a3​+…

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the alternating series test?

An alternating series of the form ∑n=1∞​(−1)n+1an​ or ∑n=1∞​(−1)nan​ converges if 0<an+1​≤an​ for all n≥1 and limn→∞​an​=0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the remainder of an alternating series?

TODO

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the relationship between absolute and conditional convergence?

A series ∑n=1∞​an​ exhibits absolute convergence if ∑n=1∞​∣an​∣ converges.

A series ∑n=1∞​an​ exhibits conditional convergence if ∑n=1∞​an​ converges but ∑n=1∞​∣an​∣ diverges.

If ∑n=1∞​∣an​∣ converges, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the ratio test?

Consider the series ∑n=1∞​an​ with nonzero terms. Let

ρ=n→∞lim​​an​an+1​​​
  • If 0≤ρ<1, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges absolutely.
  • If ρ>1 or ρ=∞, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.
  • If ρ=1, the test does not provide any information.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the root test?

Consider the series ∑n=1∞​an​. Let

ρ=n→∞lim​n∣an​∣​
  • If 0≤ρ<1, then ∑n=1∞​an​ converges absolutely.
  • If ρ>1 or ρ=∞, then ∑n=1∞​an​ diverges.
  • If ρ=1, the test does not provide any information.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the power series?

The power series centered at x=0 is an infinite series of the following form

n=0∑∞​cn​xn=c0​+c1​x+c2​x2+…

The power series centered at x=a is an infinite series of the following form

n=0∑∞​cn​(x−a)n=c0​+c1​(x−a)+c2​(x−a)2+…

where x is a variable and the coefficients cn​ are constants.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the center of a power series?

The center of the power series ∑n=0∞​cn​(x−a)n is the point x=a, the value around which the series is expanded.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What are the convergence properties of a power series?

Consider the power series ∑n=0∞​cn​(x−a)n. The series satisfies exactly one of the following properties:

  • The series converges at x=a and diverges for all x=a.
  • The series converges for all real numbers x.
  • There exists a real number R>0 such that the series converges if ∣x−a∣<R and diverges if ∣x−a∣>R. At the values x where ∣x−a∣=R, the series may converge or diverge.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Sequences and Series

Tags: Math Calculus Sequences-and-Series Theory

What is the interval of convergence of a power series?

The interval of convergence I of the power series ∑n=0∞​cn​(x−a)n is the set of all real numbers x such that the series converges. It's the "domain" of the power series.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors Theory

What is the algebraic definition the dot product?

Consider two vectors a and b in an n-dimensional space a=[a1​,a2​,…,an​], b=[b1​,b2​,…,bn​].

The dot product of a and b is computed as the sum of the products of the corresponding components:

a⋅b=a1​b1​+a2​b2​+…+an​bn​=i=1∑n​ai​bi​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Vector Calculus

Tags: Math Calculus Vectors Theory

What is the geometric definition the dot product?

Consider two vectors a and b in an n-dimensional space a=[a1​,a2​,…,an​], b=[b1​,b2​,…,bn​].

The dot product of a and b is defined in terms of the magnitudes (lengths) of the vectors and the angle between them:

a⋅b=∥a∥∥b∥cosθ

where

  • ∥a∥ is the magnitude (length) of vector a.
  • ∥a∥ is the magnitude (length) of vector b.
  • θ is the angle between the two vectors.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::ODE

Tags: Math Calculus Differential-Equation ODE Theory

What is an ordinary differential equation (ODE)?

An ordinary differential equation is a mathematical equation that relates an unknown function of a single independent variable to its derivatives with respect to that variable.

A general n-th order ordinary differential equation can be expressed as

F(x,y,y′,y′′,…,y(n))=0

where

  • x is the independent variable.
  • y is the unknown function of x, i.e., y=y(x).
  • y′,y′′,…,y(n) are the first, second, ..., n-th derivatives of y with respect to x.
  • F is a given function relating all these variables and derivatives.

The solution for a differential equation is a function which satisfies the above expression.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the definition of the Laplace transform?

The Laplace transform of a function f(t) is a function F(s) defined as

F(s)=L{f}=∫0∞​e−stf(t)dt

where f(t) is a function of exponential order defined for all t≥0.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the first translation or shifting property of the Laplace transform?

If f(t) has the transform F(s) then

L{ectf(t)}=F(s−c) L−1{F(s−c)}=ectf(t)

Example:

Since

L{cos2t}=s2+4s​

we have

L{e3tcos2t}=F(s−3)=(s−3)2+4(s−3)​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the second translation or shifting property of the Laplace transform?

If f(t) has the transform F(s) then

L{f(t−c)u(t−c)}=e−csF(s) L−1{e−csF(s)}=f(t−c)u(t−c)

where u(t−c) is the unit step function, which is defined as

u(t−c)={10​t≥ct<c​

Example:

Since

L{t3}=s46​

we have

L{f(t−2)u(t−2)}=e−2sF(s)=e−2ss46​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the change of scale property of the Laplace transform?

If f(t) has the transform F(s) then

L{f(ct)}=c1​F(cs​) L−1{c1​F(cs​)}=f(ct)

Example:

Since

L{sint}=s2+11​

we have

L{sin3t}=31​F(3s​)=31​(s/3)2+11​=s2+93​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the unit step function u(t−c)?

The unit step function (Heaviside function) u(t−c) is defined as

u(t−c)={10​t≥ct<c​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the effect of multiplying by the unit step function u(t−c)?

Let f(t)=0 for all t<0.

  • f(t)u(t−c) with c>0 is f(t) switched on at the point c.
  • f(t−c)u(t−c) with c>0 is f(t) shifted (translated) to the right by the amount c.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the unit impulse function δ(t−c)?

The unit impulse function (Dirac delta function) δ(t−c) is a function that has the following properties.

Infinite at the origin c, zero everywhere else:

δ(t−c)={∞0​t=ct=c​

Area of unity:

∫0∞​δ(t−c)dt=1

Shifting or sampling property:

∫−∞∞​f(t)δ(t−c)dt=f(c)

The unit impulse function is technically not a function but a generalized function or distribution.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Laplace Transform

Tags: Math Calculus Laplace-Transform Theory

What is the derivative of the unit step function?

The derivative of the unit step function (Heaviside function) is the unit impulse function (Dirac delta function);

dtd​u(t)=δ(t)

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::PDE

Tags: Math Calculus Differential-Equation PDE Theory

What is an partial differential equation (PDE)?

A partial differential equation is a mathematical equation that involves an unknown function of multiple independent variables and its partial derivatives with respect to those variables.

A general k-th order partial differential equation involving an unknown function u of n independent variables x1​,x2​,…,xn​ can be expressed as

F(x1​,x2​,…,xn​,u,∂x1​∂u​,…,∂xn​∂u​,∂x12​∂2u​,…,∂xi1​​∂xi2​​…∂xik​​∂ku​)=0

where

  • x1​,x2​,…,xn​ are the independent variables.
  • u=u(x1​,x2​,…,xn​) is the unknown function.
  • ∂xi1​​∂xi2​​…∂xik​​∂ku​ denotes a k-th order partial derivative.
  • F is a given function relating all these variables and derivatives.

The solution for a differential equation is a function which satisfies the above expression.

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫3x2x3+1​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=x3+1=3x2dx​

Then:

∫3x2x3+1​dx​=∫u​du=32​u23​+C=32​(x3+1)23​+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫xe−x2dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu−21​du​=−x2=−2xdx=xdx​

Then:

∫xe−x2dx​=−21​∫eudu=−21​eu+C=−21​e−x2+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫(x2+1)2x​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu21​du​=x2+1=2xdx=xdx​

Then:

∫(x2+1)2x​dx​=21​∫u21​du=−21​u1​+C=−21​x2+11​+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫ecosxsinxdx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu−du​=cosx=−sinxdx=sinxdx​

Then:

∫ecosxsinxdx​=−∫eudu=−eu+C=−ecosx+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫ex+1ex​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=ex+1=exdx​

Then:

∫ex+1ex​dx​=∫u1​du=ln∣u∣+C=ln(ex+1)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫x2+1x​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu21​du​=x2+1=2xdx=xdx​

Then:

∫x2+1x​dx​=21​∫u1​du=21​ln∣u∣+C=21​ln(x2+1)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫1+cos2xsinx​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu−du​=cosx=−sinxdx=sinxdx​

Then:

∫1+cos2xsinx​dx​=−∫1+u21​du=−tan−1u+C=−tan−1(cosx)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫cos(x)sin(sin(x))dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=sinx=cosxdx​

Then:

∫cos(x)sin(sin(x))dx​=∫sin(u)du=−cos(u)+C=−cos(sin(x))+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫xlnx​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=lnx=x1​dx​

Then:

∫xlnx​dx​=∫udu=21​u2+C=21​(lnx)2+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫xlnx1​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=lnx=x1​dx​

Then:

∫xlnx1​dx​=∫u1​du=ln∣u∣+C=ln∣lnx∣+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫x+1​x​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

xudu​=u−1=x+1=dx​

Then:

∫x+1​x​dx​=∫u​u−1​du=∫u​u​du−∫u​1​du=∫u1/2du−∫u−1/2du=32​u23​−2u21​+C=32​(x+1)23​−2(x+1)21​+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫1+x4x​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

udu21​du​=x2=2xdx=xdx​

Then:

∫1+x4x​dx​=21​∫1+u21​du=21​tan−1(u)+C=21​tan−1(x2)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫(x+1)x−1​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

xudu​=u+1=x−1=dx​

Then:

∫(x+1)x−1​dx​=∫(u+2)u​du=∫(uu​+2u​)du=∫(u23​+2u21​)du=∫u23​du+2∫u21​du=52​u25​+34​u23​+C=52​(x−1)25​+34​(x−1)23​+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫x2+4x+81​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution after completing the square:

x2+4x+8udu​=(x+2)2+22=x+2=dx​

Then:

∫x2+4x+81​dx​=∫u2+221​du=21​tan−1(2u​)+C=21​tan−1(2x+2​)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫1+2x​1​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

ududx​=1+2x​=1+(2x)21​=(2x)−21​dx=(2x)21​du​

Then:

∫1+2x​1​dx​=∫u1​(2x)21​du,(2x)21​=u−1=∫u1​(u−1)du=∫uu​−u1​du=∫du−∫u1​du=u−ln∣u∣+C=(1+2x​)−ln(1+2x​)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫ex​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

ududx​=x​=2x​1​dx=2u1​dx=2udu​

Then:

∫ex​dx​=2∫ueudu=2ueu−2eu+C=2eu(u−1)+C=2ex​(x​−1)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral U-Substitution

∫1+x​1​dx

Solve the integral.

Apply u-substitution:

ududx​=x​=2x​1​dx=2u1​dx=2udu​

Then:

∫1+x​1​dx​=2∫u+1u​du=2∫u+1u+1−1​du=2∫1−u+11​du=2(u−ln∣u+1∣)+C=2(x​−ln∣x​+1∣)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Integration-by-Parts

∫lnxdx

Solve the integral.

Integrate by parts:

udv=uv−∫vdu udu​=lnx=x1​dx​dvv​=dx=x​

Then:

∫lnxdx​=xlnx−∫xx1​dx=xlnx−∫dx=xlnx−x+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Integration-by-Parts

∫(lnx)2dx

Solve the integral.

Integrate by parts:

udv=uv−∫vdu udu​=(lnx)2=x2lnx​dx​dvv​=dx=x​

Then:

∫(lnx)2dx​=x(lnx)2−∫xx2lnx​dx=x(lnx)2−2∫lnxdx=x(lnx)2−2(xlnx−x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫sin2(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

sin2(x)=21−cos(2x)​

Then:

∫sin2(x)dx​=∫21−cos(2x)​dx=∫21​−21​cos(2x)dx=21​x−41​sin(2x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫cos2(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

cos2(x)=21+cos(2x)​

Then:

∫cos2(x)dx​=∫21+cos(2x)​dx=∫21​+21​cos(2x)dx=21​x+41​sin(2x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫sin3(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

sin2(x)=1−cos2(2)

Then:

∫sin3(x)dx​=∫sin2(x)sin(x)dx=∫(1−cos2(x))sin(x)dx​

Apply u-substitution:

u−du​=cos(x)=sin(x)dx​

Then:

∫(1−cos2(x))sin(x)dx​=∫−(1−u2)du=∫u2−1du=31​u3−u+C=31​cos3(x)−cos(x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫cos3(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

cos2(x)=1−sin2(2)

Then:

∫cos3(x)dx​=∫cos2(x)cos(x)dx=∫(1−sin2(x))cos(x)dx​

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=sin(x)=cos(x)dx​

Then:

∫(1−sin2(x))cos(x)dx​=∫1−u2du=u−31​u3+C=sin(x)−31​sin3(x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫sin4(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

sin2(x)=21−cos(2x)​

and

cos2(2x)=21+cos(4x)​

Then:

∫sin4(x)dx​=∫[21−cos(2x)​]2dx=∫41​[1−2cos(2x)+cos2(2x)]dx=∫41​[1−2cos(2x)+21​(1+cos(4x))]dx=∫41​[23​−2cos(2x)+21​cos(4x)]dx=∫83​−21​cos(2x)+81​cos(4x)dx=83​x−41​sin(2x)+321​sin(4x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫cos4(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

cos2(x)=21+cos(2x)​

and

cos2(2x)=21+cos(4x)​

Then:

∫cos4(x)dx​=∫[21+cos(2x)​]2dx=∫41​[1+2cos(2x)+cos2(2x)]dx=∫41​[1+2cos(2x)+21​(1+cos(4x))]dx=∫41​[23​+2cos(2x)+21​cos(4x)]dx=∫83​+21​cos(2x)+81​cos(4x)dx=83​x+41​sin(2x)+321​sin(4x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫cos2(x)sin3(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

sin2(x)=1−cos2(x)

Then:

∫cos2(x)sin3(x)dx​=∫cos2(x)sin2(x)sin(x)dx=∫cos2(x)(1−cos2(x))sin(x)dx​

Apply u-substitution:

u−du​=cos(x)=sin(x)dx​

Then:

∫cos2(x)(1−cos2(x))sin(x)dx​=∫−u2(1−u2)du=∫u4−u2du=51​u5−31​u3+C=51​cos5(x)−31​cos3(x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫sin2(x)cos3(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

cos2(x)=1−sin2(x)

Then:

∫sin2(x)cos3(x)dx​=∫sin2(x)cos2(x)cos(x)dx=∫sin2(x)(1−sin2(x))cos(x)dx​

Apply u-substitution:

udu​=sin(x)=cos(x)​

Then:

∫sin2(x)(1−sin2(x))cos(x)dx​=∫u2(1−u2)du=∫u2−u4du=31​u3−51​u5+C=31​sin3(x)−51​sin5(x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫sin2(x)cos2(x)dx

Solve the integral.

Apply trigonometric identity:

sin(2x)=2sin(x)cos(x)

and

sin2(2x)=21−cos(4x)​

Then:

∫sin2(x)cos2(x)dx​=∫sin(x)sin(x)cos(x)cos(x)dx=∫41​(2sin(x)cos(x))(2sin(x)cos(x))dx=∫41​sin(2x)sin(2x)dx=∫41​sin2(2x)dx=∫81​(1−cos(4x))dx=81​(∫dx−∫cos(4x)dx)=81​(x−41​sin(4x))+C=81​x−321​sin(4x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Integrals

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Integral Trigonometric

∫1+sin(x)1​dx

Solve the integral.

∫1+sin(x)1​dx​=∫(1+sin(x)1​)(1−sin(x)1−sin(x)​)dx=∫1−sin2(x)1−sin(x)​dx=∫cos2(x)1−sin(x)​dx=∫cos2(x)1​−cos2(x)sin(x)​dx=∫sec2(x)−tan(x)sec(x)dx=tan(x)−sec(x)+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Lambert W

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Lambert-W

dxd​W(x)

Find the derivative of the Lambert W function.

Use implicit differentiation:

W(x)eW(x)dxd​(W(x)eW(x))​=x=dxd​x​

Use the chain rule:

W(x)eW(x)⋅W′(x)+eW(x)⋅W′(x)W′(x)(W(x)eW(x)+eW(x))W′(x)(x+eW(x))​=1=1=1​

Since

W(x)eW(x)=x⟹eW(x)=x/W(x)

Then:

W′(x)​=x+eW(x)1​=x+x/W(x)1​=xW(x)+xW(x)​=x(W(x)+1)W(x)​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Lambert W

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Lambert-W

∫W(x)dx

Find the anti-derivative of the Lambert W function.

Apply u-substitution:

uxdx​=W(x)=ueu=(ueu+eu)du=eu(u+1)du​

Then:

​=∫W(x)dx=∫ueu(u+1)du=∫eu(u2+u)du,Integrate by parts=(u2+u)eu−(2u+1)eu+2eu+C=uueu−ueu+eu+C=xW(x)−W(x)+eW(x)+C=xW(x)−W(x)+W(x)x​+C​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Lambert W

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Lambert-W

W(W(x))=1
W(W(x))W(x)x​=1=1e1=e=eee=ee+1​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Lambert W

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Lambert-W

W(x)=ln(2x)
W(x)x​=ln(2x)=ln(2x)eln(2x)=2xln(2x)​ 2xln(2x)ln(2x)eln(2x)2xx​=x=21​=e21​=e21​=21​e21​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Calculus::Problems::Lambert W

Tags: Math Calculus Problem Lambert-W

W(ex)=(W(e))x
W(ex)W(ex)W(ex)W(ex)exx​=(W(e))x=(W(1e1))x=(1)x=1=1e1=1​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Extra

Tags: Math Algebra Mean

The definition of the Arithmetic Mean (AM)

The Arithmetic Mean (AM) of two numbers a and b is given by:

AM=2a+b​

The general formula for n numbers x1​,x2​,…,xn​ is:

AM=nx1​+x2​+…+xn​​=n1​i=1∑n​xi​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Extra

Tags: Math Algebra Mean

The definition of the Geometric Mean (GM)

The Geometric Mean (GM) of two numbers a and b is given by:

GM=ab​

The general formula for n numbers x1​,x2​,…,xn​ is:

GM=(x1​⋅x2​⋅…⋅xn​)1/n=(i=1∏n​xi​)1/n

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Extra

Tags: Math Algebra Mean

The definition of the Quadratic Mean (QM)

The Quadratic Mean (QM) of two numbers a and b is given by:

QM=2a2+b2​​

This is also known as the Root Mean Square (RMS).

The general formula for n numbers x1​,x2​,…,xn​ is:

QM=nx12​+x22​+…+xn2​​​=n1​i=1∑n​xi2​​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Extra

Tags: Math Algebra Mean

The definition of the Harmonic Mean (HM)

The Harmonic Mean (HM) of two numbers a and b is given by:

HM=1/a+1/b2​=a+b2ab​

The general formula for n numbers x1​,x2​,…,xn​ is:

HM=x1​1​+x2​1​+…+xn​1​n​=∑i=1n​xi​1​n​

Type: Basic Math::Card 1::Back

Deck: Math::Extra

Tags: Math Algebra Mean

AM-GM Inequality

2a+b​≥ab​

where AM is the Arithmetic Mean and GM is the Geometric Mean of two non-negative numbers a and b.

Extra

More generally, it is known that the following inequality holds for any set of positive numbers:

QM≥AM≥GM≥HM

where

  • QM is the Quadratic Mean
  • AM is the Arithmetic Mean
  • GM is the Geometric Mean
  • HM is the Harmonic Mean
